articles.title: Cisco Patches CVE-2026-20230 in Unified CM as Exploit Code Goes Public
articles.summary: Cisco has patched a bug in Unified Communications Manager that lets an unauthenticated attacker on the network write files to the box and, from there, climb to root. It is tracked as CVE-2026-20230, and proof-of-concept exploit code is already public. Cisco's PSIRT says it has not seen the flaw used in attacks yet. The PoC shortens that runway. The flaw is a server-side request forgery.

keypoints: Unauthenticated network attackers exploit SSRF for root access
Public exploit code accelerates attack timeline significantly

analysis.title: Vulnerability Details
analysis.desc: Server-side request forgery in Cisco Unified CM enables file write and privilege escalation to root without authentication.

analysis.desc: Public proof-of-concept code reduces time before widespread exploitation, despite no confirmed in-the-wild attacks yet.

analysis.title: Mitigation Priority
analysis.desc: Network-accessible Unified CM installations require immediate patching to prevent unauthenticated remote code execution.

articles.title: Hackers Spied on a Stock Exchange Executive's Outlook Mailbox for Five Months

articles.summary: Redis has patched a use-after-free in its blocking-client code that lets an authenticated user run arbitrary OS commands on the machine hosting the database. The flaw was found by an autonomous AI tool built to hunt bugs in large codebases. Tracked as CVE-2026-23479, the flaw was introduced in Redis 7.2.0 and remained in every stable branch until the May 5 fixes, unnoticed for over two years.

articles.title: Beyond the Zero-Day: See Your Network Like an Attacker | Webinar with HD Moore
